Poet  ·  German-American Voice

Nanette Schieron

Nanette Schieron is a German-American poet whose work explores memory, loss, resilience, and the experience of building a life between countries. A former psychotherapist, she brings both lyrical precision and deep human attentiveness to themes of identity, nature, and the enduring power of the past.

Her poems have appeared in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily and Haikuniverse, and she has been one of several featured poets at the Duxbury Art Complex Museum in Duxbury, MA — writing and reading original ekphrastic poems before an audience for four consecutive years. Nanette has been a featured poet at the Rockland Memorial Library, Rockland, MA, in addition to reading her work at other local libraries.

She writes with a close attentiveness to the world — its textures, its losses, and its unexpected moments of grace.

  • Poems
  • Readings
  • Themes
  • Contact

Selected Work

The Lavender Dress

Read ↓

Memory  ·  Personal History

My hands shaking, I brushed my hair, shadowed my eyes then slipped on the lavender dress — purple poppies blooming on soft Indian cotton — a dress too delicate for heartbreak.
It was my thirtieth birthday. My gold wedding band flashing in late afternoon sun, I drove the wildflower-meadowed country road north — the one that once led me home to you.
Steeped in summersweet, the breeze carried the memory of a first kiss — how you asked, how I said yes, our shyness giving way to desire with the press of our lips.
Still in reverie, I entered the waiting room. You looked up — no happy birthday wish no comment on the dress I wore for you. Your face, a gray wall; those deep blue eyes like locked windows before a storm.
You left the hour early, taking all the air with you. I called you coward, gasped for breath between sobs — cheeks burning. The counselor, her eyes soft handed me a tissue box.
The lavender dress tied at my nape felt suddenly tight — poppies, damp and dark, holding wordless sorrow.
My tear-streaked face met the cool night air — Above me a violet-blue sky opened — I drew a long, slow breath, started the car — as the moon rose spilling its silver light on the road ahead.

A version of this poem was published in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily.

Invitation

Read ↓

Nature  ·  Ekphrastic

Walk with me this warm spring evening as twilight washes into dusk, shrouding the river's blue with a deep pewter hue — across the burgeoning grasses, a heron's raspy call.
Come — we'll wander the tidal flats, follow mysterious animal tracks as our footsteps sink into the spongy layers. We'll drink in the long breath of eons that mingles with our own.
The moon will rise later tonight. Silver stars catch in the crowns of wind-blown pines and sequin the water's darkening skin.
Above, the hunter with his bow, watches over us, as the modern world drifts by.

Haiku

Read ↓

Form  ·  Nature

abalone moon casting jewels on the lake lawn green with envy
cooing mourning doves in the weeping spruce prayers for the world
echoes in red canyon soft notes of native flute wild horses appear
geese flying low salt marsh wakes blizzard of white
hydrangea's blue gown beaded with gold bees fashion week

Readings & Performances

  • Duxbury Art Complex Museum

    Duxbury, MA  ·  Ekphrastic poems written and performed before a live audience  ·  Four consecutive years

  • Local Library Reading Series

Poetry Themes

Memory Loss Resilience Immigration & Identity The Natural World Marriage & Relationships Dreams Ekphrasis The German-American Experience

Readings, collaborations,
and correspondence welcome.

For inquiries about readings, ekphrastic commissions, or to connect about the work, please reach out by email.

padding: 80px 48px 64px; overflow: hidden; } header::before { content: ''; position: absolute; inset: 0; background: radial-gradient(ellipse 60% 50% at 80% 20%, rgba(180,150,210,0.18) 0%, transparent 70%), radial-gradient(ellipse 40% 40% at 10% 80%, rgba(155,122,184,0.12) 0%, transparent 60%); pointer-events: none; } .header-inner { max-width: 860px; margin: 0 auto; position: relative; display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr auto; column-gap: 56px; align-items: start; } .header-text { grid-column: 1; } .photo-block { grid-column: 2; grid-row: 1; align-self: center; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.9s 0.6s forwards; } .photo-placeholder { width: 190px; height: 230px; border-radius: 4px; background: linear-gradient(160deg, #ddd5eb, #c9b8d8); display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center; justify-content: center; gap: 12px; border: 1px dashed var(--lavender-dk); cursor: pointer; transition: background 0.2s; } .photo-placeholder:hover { background: linear-gradient(160deg, #d0c4e3, #bda8d0); } .photo-placeholder svg { opacity: 0.45; } .photo-placeholder span { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); text-align: center; padding: 0 12px; line-height: 1.6; } .photo-actual { width: 190px; height: 230px; border-radius: 4px; object-fit: cover; display: none; border: 1px solid var(--lavender); } .eyebrow { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-weight: 300; font-size: 11px; letter-spacing: 4px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); margin-bottom: 20px; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.8s 0.1s forwards; } h1 { font-size: clamp(2.6rem, 5.5vw, 4.5rem); font-weight: 300; font-style: italic; line-height: 1.1; color: var(--ink); letter-spacing: -0.5px; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.9s 0.25s forwards; } .header-rule { width: 64px; height: 1px; background: var(--lavender-dk); margin: 28px 0; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.8s 0.4s forwards; } .bio { max-width: 560px; font-size: 1.1rem; line-height: 1.9; font-weight: 300; color: var(--ink); opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.9s 0.55s forwards; } .bio strong { font-weight: 600; } /* ── NAV ─────────────────────────────────── */ nav { background: var(--warm-white); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); border-bottom: 1px solid var(--rule); padding: 0 48px; position: sticky; top: 0; z-index: 100; } nav ul { max-width: 860px; margin: 0 auto; list-style: none; display: flex; gap: 40px; } nav a { display: block; padding: 18px 0; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 400; letter-spacing: 3px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--muted); text-decoration: none; border-bottom: 2px solid transparent; transition: color 0.2s, border-color 0.2s; } nav a:hover { color: var(--lavender-dk); border-bottom-color: var(--lavender-dk); } /* ── SECTIONS ────────────────────────────── */ .section-wrap { max-width: 860px; margin: 0 auto; padding: 80px 48px; } .section-divider { border: none; border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } .section-label { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-weight: 300; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 5px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); margin-bottom: 48px; } /* ── POEMS ───────────────────────────────── */ .poems-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 64px; } .poem-card::before { content: ''; display: block; width: 2px; height: 40px; background: linear-gradient(to bottom, var(--lavender-dk), transparent); margin-bottom: 24px; } .poem-title { font-size: 1.35rem; font-weight: 400; font-style: italic; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 6px; } .poem-note { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2.5px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--muted); margin-bottom: 28px; } .poem-stanza { font-size: 1rem; line-height: 2; font-weight: 300; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 1.6em; white-space: pre-line; } .poem-attribution { margin-top: 28px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2px; color: var(--muted); } /* ── READINGS ────────────────────────────── */ .readings-list { list-style: none; display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 32px; } .reading-item { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 2px 1fr; gap: 20px; align-items: start; } .reading-bar { width: 2px; height: 100%; min-height: 44px; background: var(--lavender); border-radius: 1px; } .reading-venue { font-size: 1.05rem; font-weight: 400; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 5px; } .reading-detail { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 300; color: var(--muted); line-height: 1.7; } /* ── THEMES ──────────────────────────────── */ .themes-wrap { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 14px; } .theme-tag { padding: 10px 22px; border: 1px solid var(--lavender); border-radius: 40px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 300; letter-spacing: 1.5px; color: var(--lavender-dk); background: rgba(201,184,216,0.08); transition: background 0.2s, border-color 0.2s; } .theme-tag:hover { background: rgba(201,184,216,0.22); border-color: var(--lavender-dk); } /* ── CONTACT ─────────────────────────────── */ .contact-bg { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #ede5f7 0%, #f5f0ea 100%); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } .contact-heading { font-size: clamp(1.5rem, 3vw, 2.2rem); font-weight: 300; font-style: italic; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 14px; } .contact-sub { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 14px; font-weight: 300; color: var(--muted); margin-bottom: 36px; line-height: 1.7; max-width: 480px; } .contact-link { display: inline-block; padding: 14px 36px; background: var(--lavender-dk); color: #fff; text-decoration: none; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; letter-spacing: 3px; text-transform: uppercase; border-radius: 2px; transition: background 0.2s, transform 0.15s; } .contact-link:hover { background: var(--ink); transform: translateY(-1px); } /* ── FOOTER ──────────────────────────────── */ footer { text-align: center; padding: 36px 24px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 11px; letter-spacing: 2px; color: var(--muted); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } /* ── ANIMATIONS ──────────────────────────── */ @keyframes fadeUp { from { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(16px); } to { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); } } .reveal { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(20px); transition: opacity 0.7s ease, transform 0.7s ease; } .reveal.visible { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); } /* ── RESPONSIVE ──────────────────────────── */ @media (max-width: 680px) { header, .section-wrap { padding-left: 24px; padding-right: 24px; } nav { padding: 0 24px; } .header-inner { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } .photo-block { grid-column: 1; margin-top: 36px; } .poems-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } } /* ── COLLAPSIBLE POEMS ───────────────────── */ .poem-card { cursor: default; } .poem-toggle { display: flex; align-items: baseline; gap: 16px; cursor: pointer; user-select: none; } .poem-toggle .poem-title { margin-bottom: 0; } .toggle-icon { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); opacity: 0.7; transition: opacity 0.2s; white-space: nowrap; margin-top: 6px; } .poem-toggle:hover .toggle-icon { opacity: 1; } .poem-body { overflow: hidden; max-height: 0; transition: max-height 0.5s ease, opacity 0.4s ease; opacity: 0; } .poem-body.open { max-height: 2000px; opacity: 1; }

Poet  ·  German-American Voice

Nanette Schieron

Nanette Schieron is a German-American poet whose work explores memory, loss, resilience, and the experience of building a life between countries. A former psychotherapist, she brings both lyrical precision and deep human attentiveness to themes of identity, nature, and the enduring power of the past.

Her poems have appeared in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily and Haikuniverse, and she has been one of several featured poets at the Duxbury Art Complex Museum in Duxbury, MA — writing and reading original poems before an audience for four consecutive years. Nanette has been a featured poet at the Rockland Memorial Library, Rockland, MA, in addition to reading her work at other local libraries.

She writes with a close attentiveness to the world — its textures, its losses, and its unexpected moments of grace.

  • Poems
  • Readings
  • Themes

Selected Work

The Lavender Dress

Read ↓

Memory  ·  Personal History

My hands shaking, I brushed my hair, shadowed my eyes then slipped on the lavender dress — purple poppies blooming on soft Indian cotton — a dress too delicate for heartbreak.
It was my thirtieth birthday. My gold wedding band flashing in late afternoon sun, I drove the wildflower-meadowed country road north — the one that once led me home to you.
Steeped in summersweet, the breeze carried the memory of a first kiss — how you asked, how I said yes, our shyness giving way to desire with the press of our lips.
Still in reverie, I entered the waiting room. You looked up — no happy birthday wish no comment on the dress I wore for you. Your face, a gray wall; those deep blue eyes like locked windows before a storm.
You left the hour early, taking all the air with you. I called you coward, gasped for breath between sobs — cheeks burning. The counselor, her eyes soft handed me a tissue box.
The lavender dress tied at my nape felt suddenly tight — poppies, damp and dark, holding wordless sorrow.
My tear-streaked face met the cool night air — Above me a violet-blue sky opened — I drew a long, slow breath, started the car — as the moon rose spilling its silver light on the road ahead.

A version of this poem was published in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily.

Nature  ·  Ekphrastic

Walk with me this warm spring evening as twilight washes into dusk, shrouding the river's blue with a deep pewter hue, across the burgeoning grasses, a heron's raspy call.
Come — we'll wander the tidal flats, follow mysterious animal tracks as our footsteps sink into the spongy layers. We'll drink in the long breath of eons that mingles with our own.
The moon will rise later tonight, silver stars already sequin the water's darkening skin, catching in the crowns of wind-blown pines.
Above, the hunter with his bow, watches over us, as the modern world drifts by.

Poet  ·  German-American Voice

Nanette Schieron

Nanette Schieron is a German-American poet whose work explores memory, loss, resilience, and the experience of building a life between countries. A former psychotherapist, she brings both lyrical precision and deep human attentiveness to themes of identity, nature, and the enduring power of the past.

Her poems have appeared in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily and Haikuniverse, and she has been one of several featured poets at the Duxbury Art Complex Museum in Duxbury, MA — writing and reading original poems before an audience for four consecutive years. She has also read her work at libraries throughout the region.

She writes with a close attentiveness to the world — its textures, its losses, and its unexpected moments of grace.

  • Poems
  • Readings
  • Themes
  • Contact

Selected Work

The Lavender Dress

Read ↓

Memory  ·  Personal History

My hands shaking, I brushed my hair, shadowed my eyes then slipped on the lavender dress — purple poppies blooming on soft Indian cotton — a dress too delicate for heartbreak.
It was my thirtieth birthday. My gold wedding band flashing in late afternoon sun, I drove the wildflower-meadowed country road north — the one that once led me home to you.
Steeped in summersweet, the breeze carried the memory of a first kiss — how you asked, how I said yes, our shyness giving way to desire with the press of our lips.
Still in reverie, I entered the waiting room. You looked up — no happy birthday wish no comment on the dress I wore for you. Your face, a gray wall; those deep blue eyes like locked windows before a storm.
You left the hour early, taking all the air with you. I called you coward, gasped for breath between sobs — cheeks burning. The counselor, her eyes soft handed me a tissue box.
The lavender dress tied at my nape felt suddenly tight — poppies, damp and dark, holding wordless sorrow.
My tear-streaked face met the cool night air — Above me a violet-blue sky opened — I drew a long, slow breath, started the car — as the moon rose spilling its silver light on the road ahead.

N. Schieron

A version of this poem was published in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily.

Invitation

Read ↓

Nature  ·  Ekphrastic

Walk with me this warm spring evening as twilight washes into dusk, shrouding the river's blue with a deep pewter hue, across the burgeoning grasses, a heron's raspy call.
Come — we'll wander the tidal flats, follow mysterious animal tracks as our footsteps sinking into the spongy layers. We'll drink in the long breath of eons that mingles with our own.
The moon rises later tonight, silver stars already sequin the water's darkening skin, catching in the crowns of wind-blown pines.
Above, the hunter with his bow, watches over us, as the modern world drifts by.

N. Schieron  ·  9.21.25

padding: 80px 48px 64px; overflow: hidden; } header::before { content: ''; position: absolute; inset: 0; background: radial-gradient(ellipse 60% 50% at 80% 20%, rgba(180,150,210,0.18) 0%, transparent 70%), radial-gradient(ellipse 40% 40% at 10% 80%, rgba(155,122,184,0.12) 0%, transparent 60%); pointer-events: none; } .header-inner { max-width: 860px; margin: 0 auto; position: relative; display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr auto; column-gap: 56px; align-items: start; } .header-text { grid-column: 1; } .photo-block { grid-column: 2; grid-row: 1; align-self: center; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.9s 0.6s forwards; } .photo-placeholder { width: 190px; height: 230px; border-radius: 4px; background: linear-gradient(160deg, #ddd5eb, #c9b8d8); display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center; justify-content: center; gap: 12px; border: 1px dashed var(--lavender-dk); cursor: pointer; transition: background 0.2s; } .photo-placeholder:hover { background: linear-gradient(160deg, #d0c4e3, #bda8d0); } .photo-placeholder svg { opacity: 0.45; } .photo-placeholder span { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); text-align: center; padding: 0 12px; line-height: 1.6; } .photo-actual { width: 190px; height: 230px; border-radius: 4px; object-fit: cover; display: none; border: 1px solid var(--lavender); } .eyebrow { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-weight: 300; font-size: 11px; letter-spacing: 4px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); margin-bottom: 20px; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.8s 0.1s forwards; } h1 { font-size: clamp(2.6rem, 5.5vw, 4.5rem); font-weight: 300; font-style: italic; line-height: 1.1; color: var(--ink); letter-spacing: -0.5px; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.9s 0.25s forwards; } .header-rule { width: 64px; height: 1px; background: var(--lavender-dk); margin: 28px 0; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.8s 0.4s forwards; } .bio { max-width: 560px; font-size: 1.1rem; line-height: 1.9; font-weight: 300; color: var(--ink); opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.9s 0.55s forwards; } .bio strong { font-weight: 600; } /* ── NAV ─────────────────────────────────── */ nav { background: var(--warm-white); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); border-bottom: 1px solid var(--rule); padding: 0 48px; position: sticky; top: 0; z-index: 100; } nav ul { max-width: 860px; margin: 0 auto; list-style: none; display: flex; gap: 40px; } nav a { display: block; padding: 18px 0; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 400; letter-spacing: 3px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--muted); text-decoration: none; border-bottom: 2px solid transparent; transition: color 0.2s, border-color 0.2s; } nav a:hover { color: var(--lavender-dk); border-bottom-color: var(--lavender-dk); } /* ── SECTIONS ────────────────────────────── */ .section-wrap { max-width: 860px; margin: 0 auto; padding: 80px 48px; } .section-divider { border: none; border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } .section-label { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-weight: 300; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 5px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); margin-bottom: 48px; } /* ── POEMS ───────────────────────────────── */ .poems-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 64px; } .poem-card::before { content: ''; display: block; width: 2px; height: 40px; background: linear-gradient(to bottom, var(--lavender-dk), transparent); margin-bottom: 24px; } .poem-title { font-size: 1.35rem; font-weight: 400; font-style: italic; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 6px; } .poem-note { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2.5px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--muted); margin-bottom: 28px; } .poem-stanza { font-size: 1rem; line-height: 2; font-weight: 300; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 1.6em; white-space: pre-line; } .poem-attribution { margin-top: 28px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2px; color: var(--muted); } /* ── READINGS ────────────────────────────── */ .readings-list { list-style: none; display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 32px; } .reading-item { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 2px 1fr; gap: 20px; align-items: start; } .reading-bar { width: 2px; height: 100%; min-height: 44px; background: var(--lavender); border-radius: 1px; } .reading-venue { font-size: 1.05rem; font-weight: 400; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 5px; } .reading-detail { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 300; color: var(--muted); line-height: 1.7; } /* ── THEMES ──────────────────────────────── */ .themes-wrap { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 14px; } .theme-tag { padding: 10px 22px; border: 1px solid var(--lavender); border-radius: 40px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 300; letter-spacing: 1.5px; color: var(--lavender-dk); background: rgba(201,184,216,0.08); transition: background 0.2s, border-color 0.2s; } .theme-tag:hover { background: rgba(201,184,216,0.22); border-color: var(--lavender-dk); } /* ── CONTACT ─────────────────────────────── */ .contact-bg { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #ede5f7 0%, #f5f0ea 100%); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } .contact-heading { font-size: clamp(1.5rem, 3vw, 2.2rem); font-weight: 300; font-style: italic; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 14px; } .contact-sub { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 14px; font-weight: 300; color: var(--muted); margin-bottom: 36px; line-height: 1.7; max-width: 480px; } .contact-link { display: inline-block; padding: 14px 36px; background: var(--lavender-dk); color: #fff; text-decoration: none; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; letter-spacing: 3px; text-transform: uppercase; border-radius: 2px; transition: background 0.2s, transform 0.15s; } .contact-link:hover { background: var(--ink); transform: translateY(-1px); } /* ── FOOTER ──────────────────────────────── */ footer { text-align: center; padding: 36px 24px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 11px; letter-spacing: 2px; color: var(--muted); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } /* ── ANIMATIONS ──────────────────────────── */ @keyframes fadeUp { from { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(16px); } to { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); } } .reveal { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(20px); transition: opacity 0.7s ease, transform 0.7s ease; } .reveal.visible { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); } /* ── RESPONSIVE ──────────────────────────── */ @media (max-width: 680px) { header, .section-wrap { padding-left: 24px; padding-right: 24px; } nav { padding: 0 24px; } .header-inner { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } .photo-block { grid-column: 1; margin-top: 36px; } .poems-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } }

Poet  ·  German-American Voice

Nanette Schieron

Nanette Schieron is a German-American poet whose work explores memory, loss, resilience, and the experience of building a life between worlds. A former psychotherapist, she brings both lyrical precision and deep human attentiveness to themes of identity, nature, and the enduring power of the past.

Her poems have appeared in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily and Haikuniverse, and she has been a featured ekphrastic poet at the Duxbury Art Complex Museum in Duxbury, MA — writing and reading original poems before an audience for four consecutive years. She has also read her work at libraries throughout the region.

She writes with a close attentiveness to the world — its textures, its losses, and its unexpected moments of grace.

  • Poems
  • Readings
  • Themes
  • Contact

Selected Work

The Lavender Dress

Memory  ·  Personal History

My hands shaking, I brushed my hair, shadowed my eyes then slipped on the lavender dress — purple poppies blooming on soft Indian cotton — a dress too delicate for heartbreak.
It was my thirtieth birthday. My gold wedding band flashing in late afternoon sun, I drove the wildflower-meadowed country road north — the one that once led me home to you.
Steeped in summersweet, the breeze carried the memory of a first kiss — how you asked, how I said yes, our shyness giving way to desire with the press of our lips.
Still in reverie, I entered the waiting room. You looked up — no happy birthday wish no comment on the dress I wore for you. Your face, a gray wall; those deep blue eyes like locked windows before a storm.
You left the hour early, taking all the air with you. I called you coward, gasped for breath between sobs — cheeks burning. The counselor, her eyes soft handed me a tissue box.
The lavender dress tied at my nape felt suddenly tight — poppies, damp and dark, holding wordless sorrow.
My tear-streaked face met the cool night air — Above me a violet-blue sky opened — I drew a long, slow breath, started the car — as the moon rose spilling its silver light on the road ahead.

N. Schieron

Invitation

Nature  ·  Ekphrastic

Walk with me this warm spring evening as twilight washes into dusk, shrouding the river's blue with a deep pewter hue, A heron's raspy call echoes across burgeoning grasses.
Come — we'll wander the tidal flats, follow mysterious animal tracks as our footsteps sinking into the spongy layers. We'll drink in the long breath of eons that mingles with our own.
The moon rises later tonight, Already silver stars sequin the water's darkening skin, catching in the crowns of wind-blown pines.
Above the hunter with his bow, watches over us, as the modern world drifts by.

N. Schieron  ·  9.21.25

Readings & Performances

  • Duxbury Art Complex Museum

    Duxbury, MA  ·  Ekphrastic poems written and performed before a live audience  ·  Four consecutive years

  • Local Library Reading Series

    Several readings at libraries throughout the region

Poetry Themes

Memory Loss Resilience Immigration & Identity The Natural World Marriage & Relationships Dreams Ekphrasis The German-American Experience

Get in Touch

Readings, collaborations,
and correspondence welcome.

For inquiries about readings, ekphrastic commissions, or to connect about the work, please reach out by email.

Write to Nanette

© 2025 Nanette Schieron  ·  All poems remain the property of the author

Nanette Schieron — Poet

Poet  ·  German-American Voice

Nanette Schieron

Nanette Schieron is a German-American poet whose work explores memory, loss, resilience, and the experience of building a life between countries. A former psychotherapist, she brings both lyrical precision and deep human attentiveness to themes of identity, nature, and the enduring power of the past.

Her poems have appeared in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily and Haikuniverse, and she has been one of several featured poets at the Duxbury Art Complex Museum in Duxbury, MA — writing and reading original ekphrastic poems before an audience for four consecutive years. Nanette has been a featured poet at the Rockland Memorial Library, Rockland, MA, in addition to reading her work at other local libraries.

She writes with a close attentiveness to the world — its textures, its losses, and its unexpected moments of grace.

  • Poems
  • Readings
  • Themes
  • Contact

Selected Work

The Lavender Dress

Read ↓

Memory  ·  Personal History

My hands shaking, I brushed my hair, shadowed my eyes then slipped on the lavender dress — purple poppies blooming on soft Indian cotton — a dress too delicate for heartbreak.
It was my thirtieth birthday. My gold wedding band flashing in late afternoon sun, I drove the wildflower-meadowed country road north — the one that once led me home to you.
Steeped in summersweet, the breeze carried the memory of a first kiss — how you asked, how I said yes, our shyness giving way to desire with the press of our lips.
Still in reverie, I entered the waiting room. You looked up — no happy birthday wish no comment on the dress I wore for you. Your face, a gray wall; those deep blue eyes like locked windows before a storm.
You left the hour early, taking all the air with you. I called you coward, gasped for breath between sobs — cheeks burning. The counselor, her eyes soft handed me a tissue box.
The lavender dress tied at my nape felt suddenly tight — poppies, damp and dark, holding wordless sorrow.
My tear-streaked face met the cool night air — Above me a violet-blue sky opened — I drew a long, slow breath, started the car — as the moon rose spilling its silver light on the road ahead.

A version of this poem was published in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily.

Invitation

Read ↓

Nature  ·  Ekphrastic

Walk with me this warm spring evening as twilight washes into dusk, shrouding the river's blue with a deep pewter hue — across the burgeoning grasses, a heron's raspy call.
Come — we'll wander the tidal flats, follow mysterious animal tracks as our footsteps sink into the spongy layers. We'll drink in the long breath of eons that mingles with our own.
The moon will rise later tonight. Silver stars catch in the crowns of wind-blown pines and sequin the water's darkening skin.
Above, the hunter with his bow, watches over us, as the modern world drifts by.

Haiku

Read ↓

Form  ·  Nature

abalone moon casting jewels on the lake lawn green with envy
cooing mourning doves in the weeping spruce prayers for the world
echoes in red canyon soft notes of native flute wild horses appear
geese flying low salt marsh wakes blizzard of white
hydrangea's blue gown beaded with gold bees fashion week

font-weight: 300; font-style: italic; line-height: 1.1; color: var(--ink); letter-spacing: -0.5px; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.9s 0.25s forwards; } .header-rule { width: 64px; height: 1px; background: var(--lavender-dk); } .bio strong { font-weight: 600; } /* ── NAV ─────────────────────────────────── */ nav { background: var(--warm-white); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); border-bottom: 1px solid var(--rule); padding: 0 48px; position: sticky; top: 0; z-index: 100; } nav ul { max-width: 860px; margin: 0 auto; list-style: none; display: flex; gap: 40px; } nav a { display: block; padding: 18px 0; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 400; letter-spacing: 3px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--muted); text-decoration: none; border-bottom: 2px solid transparent; transition: color 0.2s, border-color 0.2s; } nav a:hover { color: var(--lavender-dk); border-bottom-color: var(--lavender-dk); } /* ── SECTIONS ────────────────────────────── */ .section-wrap { max-width: 860px; margin: 0 auto; padding: 80px 48px; } .section-divider { border: none; border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } .section-label { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-weight: 300; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 5px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); margin-bottom: 48px; } /* ── POEMS ───────────────────────────────── */ .poems-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 64px; } .poem-card::before { content: ''; display: block; width: 2px; height: 40px; background: linear-gradient(to bottom, var(--lavender-dk), transparent); margin-bottom: 24px; } .poem-title { font-size: 1.35rem; font-weight: 400; font-style: italic; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 6px; } .poem-note { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2.5px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--muted); margin-bottom: 28px; } .poem-stanza { font-size: 1rem; line-height: 2; font-weight: 300; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 1.6em; white-space: pre-line; } .poem-attribution { margin-top: 28px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2px; color: var(--muted); } /* ── READINGS ────────────────────────────── */ .readings-list { list-style: none; display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 32px; } .reading-item { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 2px 1fr; gap: 20px; align-items: start; } .reading-bar { width: 2px; height: 100%; min-height: 44px; background: var(--lavender); border-radius: 1px; } .reading-venue { font-size: 1.05rem; font-weight: 400; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 5px; } .reading-detail { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 300; color: var(--muted); line-height: 1.7; } /* ── THEMES ──────────────────────────────── */ .themes-wrap { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 14px; } .theme-tag { padding: 10px 22px; border: 1px solid var(--lavender); border-radius: 40px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 300; letter-spacing: 1.5px; color: var(--lavender-dk); background: rgba(201,184,216,0.08); transition: background 0.2s, border-color 0.2s; } .theme-tag:hover { background: rgba(201,184,216,0.22); border-color: var(--lavender-dk); } /* ── CONTACT ─────────────────────────────── */ .contact-bg { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #ede5f7 0%, #f5f0ea 100%); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } .contact-heading { font-size: clamp(1.5rem, 3vw, 2.2rem); font-weight: 300; font-style: italic; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 14px; } .contact-sub { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 14px; font-weight: 300; color: var(--muted); margin-bottom: 36px; line-height: 1.7; max-width: 480px; } .contact-link { display: inline-block; padding: 14px 36px; background: var(--lavender-dk); color: #fff; text-decoration: none; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; letter-spacing: 3px; text-transform: uppercase; border-radius: 2px; transition: background 0.2s, transform 0.15s; } .contact-link:hover { background: var(--ink); transform: translateY(-1px); } /* ── FOOTER ──────────────────────────────── */ footer { text-align: center; padding: 36px 24px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 11px; letter-spacing: 2px; color: var(--muted); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } /* ── ANIMATIONS ──────────────────────────── */ @keyframes fadeUp { from { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(16px); } to { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); } } .reveal { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(20px); transition: opacity 0.7s ease, transform 0.7s ease; } .reveal.visible { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); } /* ── RESPONSIVE ──────────────────────────── */ @media (max-width: 680px) { header, .section-wrap { padding-left: 24px; padding-right: 24px; } nav { padding: 0 24px; } .header-inner { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } .photo-block { grid-column: 1; margin-top: 36px; } .poems-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } } /* ── COLLAPSIBLE POEMS ───────────────────── */ .poem-card { cursor: default; } .poem-toggle { display: flex; align-items: baseline; gap: 16px; cursor: pointer; user-select: none; } .poem-toggle .poem-title { margin-bottom: 0; } .toggle-icon { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); opacity: 0.7; transition: opacity 0.2s; white-space: nowrap; margin-top: 6px; } .poem-toggle:hover .toggle-icon { opacity: 1; } .poem-body { overflow: hidden; max-height: 0; transition: max-height 0.5s ease, opacity 0.4s ease; opacity: 0; } .poem-body.open { max-height: 2000px; opacity: 1; }

Poet  ·  German-American Voice

Nanette Schieron

Nanette Schieron is a German-American poet whose work explores memory, loss, resilience, and the experience of building a life between countries. A former psychotherapist, she brings both lyrical precision and deep human attentiveness to themes of identity, nature, and the enduring power of the past.

Her poems have appeared in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily and Haikuniverse, and she has been one of several featured poets at the Duxbury Art Complex Museum in Duxbury, MA — writing and reading original poems before an audience for four consecutive years. Nanette has been a featured poet at the Rockland Memorial Library, Rockland, MA, in addition to reading her work at other local libraries.

She writes with a close attentiveness to the world — its textures, its losses, and its unexpected moments of grace.

  • Poems
  • Readings
  • Themes
  • Contact

Selected Work

The Lavender Dress

Read ↓

Memory  ·  Personal History

My hands shaking, I brushed my hair, shadowed my eyes then slipped on the lavender dress — purple poppies blooming on soft Indian cotton — a dress too delicate for heartbreak.
It was my thirtieth birthday. My gold wedding band flashing in late afternoon sun, I drove the wildflower-meadowed country road north — the one that once led me home to you.
Steeped in summersweet, the breeze carried the memory of a first kiss — how you asked, how I said yes, our shyness giving way to desire with the press of our lips.
Still in reverie, I entered the waiting room. You looked up — no happy birthday wish no comment on the dress I wore for you. Your face, a gray wall; those deep blue eyes like locked windows before a storm.
You left the hour early, taking all the air with you. I called you coward, gasped for breath between sobs — cheeks burning. The counselor, her eyes soft handed me a tissue box.
The lavender dress tied at my nape felt suddenly tight — poppies, damp and dark, holding wordless sorrow.
My tear-streaked face met the cool night air — Above me a violet-blue sky opened — I drew a long, slow breath, started the car — as the moon rose spilling its silver light on the road ahead.

A version of this poem was published in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily.

Invitation

Read ↓

Nature  ·  Ekphrastic

Walk with me this warm spring evening as twilight washes into dusk, shrouding the river's blue with a deep pewter hue, across the burgeoning grasses, a heron's raspy call.
Come — we'll wander the tidal flats, follow mysterious animal tracks as our footsteps sink into the spongy layers. We'll drink in the long breath of eons that mingles with our own.
The moon will rise later tonight, silver stars already sequin the water's darkening skin, catching in the crowns of wind-blown pines.
Above, the hunter with his bow, watches over us, as the modern world drifts by.

} .photo-block { grid-column: 2; grid-row: 1; align-self: center; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.9s 0.6s forwards; } .photo-placeholder { width: 190px; height: 230px; border-radius: 4px; background: linear-gradient(160deg, #ddd5eb, #c9b8d8); display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center; justify-content: center; gap: 12px; border: 1px dashed var(--lavender-dk); cursor: pointer; transition: background 0.2s; } .photo-placeholder:hover { background: linear-gradient(160deg, #d0c4e3, #bda8d0); } .photo-placeholder svg { opacity: 0.45; } .photo-placeholder span { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); text-align: center; padding: 0 12px; line-height: 1.6; } .photo-actual { width: 190px; height: 230px; border-radius: 4px; object-fit: cover; display: none; border: 1px solid var(--lavender); } .eyebrow { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-weight: 300; font-size: 11px; letter-spacing: 4px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); margin-bottom: 20px; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.8s 0.1s forwards; } h1 { font-size: clamp(2.6rem, 5.5vw, 4.5rem); font-weight: 300; font-style: italic; line-height: 1.1; color: var(--ink); letter-spacing: -0.5px; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.9s 0.25s forwards; } .header-rule { width: 64px; height: 1px; background: var(--lavender-dk); margin: 28px 0; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.8s 0.4s forwards; } .bio { max-width: 560px; font-size: 1.1rem; line-height: 1.9; font-weight: 300; color: var(--ink); opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.9s 0.55s forwards; } .bio strong { font-weight: 600; } /* ── NAV ─────────────────────────────────── */ nav { background: var(--warm-white); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); border-bottom: 1px solid var(--rule); padding: 0 48px; position: sticky; top: 0; z-index: 100; } nav ul { max-width: 860px; margin: 0 auto; list-style: none; display: flex; gap: 40px; } nav a { display: block; padding: 18px 0; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 400; letter-spacing: 3px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--muted); text-decoration: none; border-bottom: 2px solid transparent; transition: color 0.2s, border-color 0.2s; } nav a:hover { color: var(--lavender-dk); border-bottom-color: var(--lavender-dk); } /* ── SECTIONS ────────────────────────────── */ .section-wrap { max-width: 860px; margin: 0 auto; padding: 80px 48px; } .section-divider { border: none; border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } .section-label { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-weight: 300; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 5px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); margin-bottom: 48px; } /* ── POEMS ───────────────────────────────── */ .poems-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 64px; } .poem-card::before { content: ''; display: block; width: 2px; height: 40px; background: linear-gradient(to bottom, var(--lavender-dk), transparent); margin-bottom: 24px; } .poem-title { font-size: 1.35rem; font-weight: 400; font-style: italic; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 6px; } .poem-note { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2.5px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--muted); margin-bottom: 28px; } .poem-stanza { font-size: 1rem; line-height: 2; font-weight: 300; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 1.6em; white-space: pre-line; } .poem-attribution { margin-top: 28px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2px; color: var(--muted); } /* ── READINGS ────────────────────────────── */ .readings-list { list-style: none; display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 32px; } .reading-item { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 2px 1fr; gap: 20px; align-items: start; } .reading-bar { width: 2px; height: 100%; min-height: 44px; background: var(--lavender); border-radius: 1px; } .reading-venue { font-size: 1.05rem; font-weight: 400; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 5px; } .reading-detail { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 300; color: var(--muted); line-height: 1.7; } /* ── THEMES ──────────────────────────────── */ .themes-wrap { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 14px; } .theme-tag { padding: 10px 22px; border: 1px solid var(--lavender); border-radius: 40px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 300; letter-spacing: 1.5px; color: var(--lavender-dk); background: rgba(201,184,216,0.08); transition: background 0.2s, border-color 0.2s; } .theme-tag:hover { background: rgba(201,184,216,0.22); border-color: var(--lavender-dk); } /* ── CONTACT ─────────────────────────────── */ .contact-bg { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #ede5f7 0%, #f5f0ea 100%); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } .contact-heading { font-size: clamp(1.5rem, 3vw, 2.2rem); font-weight: 300; font-style: italic; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 14px; } .contact-sub { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 14px; font-weight: 300; color: var(--muted); margin-bottom: 36px; line-height: 1.7; max-width: 480px; } .contact-link { display: inline-block; padding: 14px 36px; background: var(--lavender-dk); color: #fff; text-decoration: none; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; letter-spacing: 3px; text-transform: uppercase; border-radius: 2px; transition: background 0.2s, transform 0.15s; } .contact-link:hover { background: var(--ink); transform: translateY(-1px); } /* ── FOOTER ──────────────────────────────── */ footer { text-align: center; padding: 36px 24px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 11px; letter-spacing: 2px; color: var(--muted); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } /* ── ANIMATIONS ──────────────────────────── */ @keyframes fadeUp { from { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(16px); } to { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); } } .reveal { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(20px); transition: opacity 0.7s ease, transform 0.7s ease; } .reveal.visible { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); } /* ── RESPONSIVE ──────────────────────────── */ @media (max-width: 680px) { header, .section-wrap { padding-left: 24px; padding-right: 24px; } nav { padding: 0 24px; } .header-inner { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } .photo-block { grid-column: 1; margin-top: 36px; } .poems-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } } /* ── COLLAPSIBLE POEMS ───────────────────── */ .poem-card { cursor: default; } .poem-toggle { display: flex; align-items: baseline; gap: 16px; cursor: pointer; user-select: none; } .poem-toggle .poem-title { margin-bottom: 0; } .toggle-icon { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); opacity: 0.7; transition: opacity 0.2s; white-space: nowrap; margin-top: 6px; } .poem-toggle:hover .toggle-icon { opacity: 1; } .poem-body { overflow: hidden; max-height: 0; transition: max-height 0.5s ease, opacity 0.4s ease; opacity: 0; } .poem-body.open { max-height: 2000px; opacity: 1; }

Poet  ·  German-American Voice

Nanette Schieron

Nanette Schieron is a German-American poet whose work explores memory, loss, resilience, and the experience of building a life between countries. A former psychotherapist, she brings both lyrical precision and deep human attentiveness to themes of identity, nature, and the enduring power of the past.

Her poems have appeared in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily and Haikuniverse, and she has been one of several featured poets at the Duxbury Art Complex Museum in Duxbury, MA — writing and reading original poems before an audience for four consecutive years. She has also read her work at libraries throughout the region.

She writes with a close attentiveness to the world — its textures, its losses, and its unexpected moments of grace.

  • Poems
  • Readings
  • Themes
  • Contact

Selected Work

The Lavender Dress

Read ↓

Memory  ·  Personal History

My hands shaking, I brushed my hair, shadowed my eyes then slipped on the lavender dress — purple poppies blooming on soft Indian cotton — a dress too delicate for heartbreak.
It was my thirtieth birthday. My gold wedding band flashing in late afternoon sun, I drove the wildflower-meadowed country road north — the one that once led me home to you.
Steeped in summersweet, the breeze carried the memory of a first kiss — how you asked, how I said yes, our shyness giving way to desire with the press of our lips.
Still in reverie, I entered the waiting room. You looked up — no happy birthday wish no comment on the dress I wore for you. Your face, a gray wall; those deep blue eyes like locked windows before a storm.
You left the hour early, taking all the air with you. I called you coward, gasped for breath between sobs — cheeks burning. The counselor, her eyes soft handed me a tissue box.
The lavender dress tied at my nape felt suddenly tight — poppies, damp and dark, holding wordless sorrow.
My tear-streaked face met the cool night air — Above me a violet-blue sky opened — I drew a long, slow breath, started the car — as the moon rose spilling its silver light on the road ahead.

N. Schieron

A version of this poem was published in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily.

Invitation

Read ↓

Nature  ·  Ekphrastic

Walk with me this warm spring evening as twilight washes into dusk, shrouding the river's blue with a deep pewter hue, across the burgeoning grasses, a heron's raspy call.
Come — we'll wander the tidal flats, follow mysterious animal tracks as our footsteps sinking into the spongy layers. We'll drink in the long breath of eons that mingles with our own.
The moon rises later tonight, silver stars already sequin the water's darkening skin, catching in the crowns of wind-blown pines.
Above, the hunter with his bow, watches over us, as the modern world drifts by.

N. Schieron  ·  9.21.25

Readings & Performances

  • Duxbury Art Complex Museum

    Duxbury, MA  ·  Ekphrastic poems written and performed before a live audience  ·  Four consecutive years

  • Local Library Reading Series

    Several readings at libraries throughout the region

Poetry Themes

Memory Loss Resilience Immigration & Identity The Natural World Marriage & Relationships Dreams Ekphrasis The German-American Experience

Get in Touch

Readings, collaborations,
and correspondence welcome.

For inquiries about readings, ekphrastic commissions, or to connect about the work, please reach out by email.

Write to Nanette

© 2025 Nanette Schieron  ·  All poems remain the property of the author

margin-bottom: 5px; } .reading-detail { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 300; color: var(--muted); line-height: 1.7; } /* ── THEMES ──────────────────────────────── */ .themes-wrap { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 14px; } .theme-tag { padding: 10px 22px; border: 1px solid var(--lavender); border-radius: 40px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 300; letter-spacing: 1.5px; color: var(--lavender-dk); background: rgba(201,184,216,0.08); transition: background 0.2s, border-color 0.2s; } .theme-tag:hover { background: rgba(201,184,216,0.22); border-color: var(--lavender-dk); } /* ── CONTACT ─────────────────────────────── */ .contact-bg { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #ede5f7 0%, #f5f0ea 100%); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } .contact-heading { font-size: clamp(1.5rem, 3vw, 2.2rem); font-weight: 300; font-style: italic; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 14px; } .contact-sub { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 14px; font-weight: 300; color: var(--muted); margin-bottom: 36px; line-height: 1.7; max-width: 480px; } .contact-link { display: inline-block; padding: 14px 36px; background: var(--lavender-dk); color: #fff; text-decoration: none; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; letter-spacing: 3px; text-transform: uppercase; border-radius: 2px; transition: background 0.2s, transform 0.15s; } .contact-link:hover { background: var(--ink); transform: translateY(-1px); } /* ── FOOTER ──────────────────────────────── */ footer { text-align: center; padding: 36px 24px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 11px; letter-spacing: 2px; color: var(--muted); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } /* ── ANIMATIONS ──────────────────────────── */ @keyframes fadeUp { from { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(16px); } to { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); } } .reveal { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(20px); transition: opacity 0.7s ease, transform 0.7s ease; } .reveal.visible { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); } /* ── RESPONSIVE ──────────────────────────── */ @media (max-width: 680px) { header, .section-wrap { padding-left: 24px; padding-right: 24px; } nav { padding: 0 24px; } .header-inner { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } .photo-block { grid-column: 1; margin-top: 36px; } .poems-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } }

Poet  ·  German-American Voice

Nanette Schieron

Nanette Schieron is a German-American poet whose work explores memory, loss, resilience, and the experience of building a life between worlds. A former psychotherapist, she brings both lyrical precision and deep human attentiveness to themes of identity, nature, and the enduring power of the past.

Her poems have appeared in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily and Haikuniverse, and she has been a featured ekphrastic poet at the Duxbury Art Complex Museum in Duxbury, MA — writing and reading original poems before an audience for four consecutive years. She has also read her work at libraries throughout the region.

She writes with a close attentiveness to the world — its textures, its losses, and its unexpected moments of grace.

  • Poems
  • Readings
  • Themes
  • Contact

Selected Work

The Lavender Dress

Memory  ·  Personal History

My hands shaking, I brushed my hair, shadowed my eyes then slipped on the lavender dress — purple poppies blooming on soft Indian cotton — a dress too delicate for heartbreak.
It was my thirtieth birthday. My gold wedding band flashing in late afternoon sun, I drove the wildflower-meadowed country road north — the one that once led me home to you.
Steeped in summersweet, the breeze carried the memory of a first kiss — how you asked, how I said yes, our shyness giving way to desire with the press of our lips.
Still in reverie, I entered the waiting room. You looked up — no happy birthday wish no comment on the dress I wore for you. Your face, a gray wall; those deep blue eyes like locked windows before a storm.
You left the hour early, taking all the air with you. I called you coward, gasped for breath between sobs — cheeks burning. The counselor, her eyes soft handed me a tissue box.
The lavender dress tied at my nape felt suddenly tight — poppies, damp and dark, holding wordless sorrow.
My tear-streaked face met the cool night air — Above me a violet-blue sky opened — I drew a long, slow breath, started the car — as the moon rose spilling its silver light on the road ahead.

N. Schieron

Invitation

Nature  ·  Ekphrastic

Walk with me this warm spring evening as twilight washes into dusk, shrouding the river's blue with a deep pewter hue, A heron's raspy call echoes across burgeoning grasses.
Come — we'll wander the tidal flats, follow mysterious animal tracks as our footsteps sinking into the spongy layers. We'll drink in the long breath of eons that mingles with our own.
The moon rises later tonight, Already silver stars sequin the water's darkening skin, catching in the crowns of wind-blown pines.
Above the hunter with his bow, watches over us, as the modern world drifts by.

N. Schieron  ·  9.21.25

© 2025 Nanette Schieron  ·  All poems remain the property of the author

Nanette Schieron — Poet

Poet  ·  German-American Voice

Nanette Schieron

Nanette Schieron is a German-American poet whose work explores memory, loss, resilience, and the experience of building a life between countries. A former psychotherapist, she brings both lyrical precision and deep human attentiveness to themes of identity, nature, and the enduring power of the past.

Her poems have appeared in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily and Haikuniverse, and she has been one of several featured poets at the Duxbury Art Complex Museum in Duxbury, MA — writing and reading original ekphrastic poems before an audience for four consecutive years. Nanette has been a featured poet at the Rockland Memorial Library, Rockland, MA, in addition to reading her work at other local libraries.

She writes with a close attentiveness to the world — its textures, its losses, and its unexpected moments of grace.

  • Poems
  • Readings
  • Themes
  • Contact

Selected Work

The Lavender Dress

Read ↓

Memory  ·  Personal History

My hands shaking, I brushed my hair, shadowed my eyes then slipped on the lavender dress — purple poppies blooming on soft Indian cotton — a dress too delicate for heartbreak.
It was my thirtieth birthday. My gold wedding band flashing in late afternoon sun, I drove the wildflower-meadowed country road north — the one that once led me home to you.
Steeped in summersweet, the breeze carried the memory of a first kiss — how you asked, how I said yes, our shyness giving way to desire with the press of our lips.
Still in reverie, I entered the waiting room. You looked up — no happy birthday wish no comment on the dress I wore for you. Your face, a gray wall; those deep blue eyes like locked windows before a storm.
You left the hour early, taking all the air with you. I called you coward, gasped for breath between sobs — cheeks burning. The counselor, her eyes soft handed me a tissue box.
The lavender dress tied at my nape felt suddenly tight — poppies, damp and dark, holding wordless sorrow.
My tear-streaked face met the cool night air — Above me a violet-blue sky opened — I drew a long, slow breath, started the car — as the moon rose spilling its silver light on the road ahead.

A version of this poem was published in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily.

Invitation

Read ↓

Nature  ·  Ekphrastic

Walk with me this warm spring evening as twilight washes into dusk, shrouding the river's blue with a deep pewter hue — across the burgeoning grasses, a heron's raspy call.
Come — we'll wander the tidal flats, follow mysterious animal tracks as our footsteps sink into the spongy layers. We'll drink in the long breath of eons that mingles with our own.
The moon will rise later tonight. Silver stars catch in the crowns of wind-blown pines and sequin the water's darkening skin.
Above, the hunter with his bow, watches over us, as the modern world drifts by.

Haiku

Read ↓

Form  ·  Nature

abalone moon casting jewels on the lake lawn green with envy
cooing mourning doves in the weeping spruce prayers for the world
echoes in red canyon soft notes of native flute wild horses appear
geese flying low salt marsh wakes blizzard of white
hydrangea's blue gown beaded with gold bees fashion week

} .photo-placeholder svg { opacity: 0.45; } .photo-placeholder span { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); text-align: center; padding: 0 12px; line-height: 1.6; } .photo-actual { width: 190px; height: 230px; border-radius: 4px; object-fit: cover; display: none; border: 1px solid var(--lavender); } .eyebrow { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-weight: 300; font-size: 11px; letter-spacing: 4px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); margin-bottom: 20px; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.8s 0.1s forwards; } h1 { font-size: clamp(2.6rem, 5.5vw, 4.5rem); font-weight: 300; font-style: italic; line-height: 1.1; color: var(--ink); letter-spacing: -0.5px; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.9s 0.25s forwards; } .header-rule { width: 64px; height: 1px; background: var(--lavender-dk); margin: 28px 0; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.8s 0.4s forwards; } .bio { max-width: 560px; font-size: 1.1rem; line-height: 1.9; font-weight: 300; color: var(--ink); opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.9s 0.55s forwards; } .bio strong { font-weight: 600; } /* ── NAV ─────────────────────────────────── */ nav { background: var(--warm-white); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); border-bottom: 1px solid var(--rule); padding: 0 48px; position: sticky; top: 0; z-index: 100; } nav ul { max-width: 860px; margin: 0 auto; list-style: none; display: flex; gap: 40px; } nav a { display: block; padding: 18px 0; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 400; letter-spacing: 3px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--muted); text-decoration: none; border-bottom: 2px solid transparent; transition: color 0.2s, border-color 0.2s; } nav a:hover { color: var(--lavender-dk); border-bottom-color: var(--lavender-dk); } /* ── SECTIONS ────────────────────────────── */ .section-wrap { max-width: 860px; margin: 0 auto; padding: 80px 48px; } .section-divider { border: none; border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } .section-label { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-weight: 300; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 5px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); margin-bottom: 48px; } /* ── POEMS ───────────────────────────────── */ .poems-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 64px; } .poem-card::before { content: ''; display: block; width: 2px; height: 40px; background: linear-gradient(to bottom, var(--lavender-dk), transparent); margin-bottom: 24px; } .poem-title { font-size: 1.35rem; font-weight: 400; font-style: italic; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 6px; } .poem-note { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2.5px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--muted); margin-bottom: 28px; } .poem-stanza { font-size: 1rem; line-height: 2; font-weight: 300; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 1.6em; white-space: pre-line; } .poem-attribution { margin-top: 28px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2px; color: var(--muted); } /* ── READINGS ────────────────────────────── */ .readings-list { list-style: none; display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 32px; } .reading-item { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 2px 1fr; gap: 20px; align-items: start; } .reading-bar { width: 2px; height: 100%; min-height: 44px; background: var(--lavender); border-radius: 1px; } .reading-venue { font-size: 1.05rem; font-weight: 400; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 5px; } .reading-detail { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 300; color: var(--muted); line-height: 1.7; } /* ── THEMES ──────────────────────────────── */ .themes-wrap { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 14px; } .theme-tag { padding: 10px 22px; border: 1px solid var(--lavender); border-radius: 40px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 300; letter-spacing: 1.5px; color: var(--lavender-dk); background: rgba(201,184,216,0.08); transition: background 0.2s, border-color 0.2s; } .theme-tag:hover { background: rgba(201,184,216,0.22); border-color: var(--lavender-dk); } /* ── CONTACT ─────────────────────────────── */ .contact-bg { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #ede5f7 0%, #f5f0ea 100%); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } .contact-heading { font-size: clamp(1.5rem, 3vw, 2.2rem); font-weight: 300; font-style: italic; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 14px; } .contact-sub { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 14px; font-weight: 300; color: var(--muted); margin-bottom: 36px; line-height: 1.7; max-width: 480px; } .contact-link { display: inline-block; padding: 14px 36px; background: var(--lavender-dk); color: #fff; text-decoration: none; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 12px; letter-spacing: 3px; text-transform: uppercase; border-radius: 2px; transition: background 0.2s, transform 0.15s; } .contact-link:hover { background: var(--ink); transform: translateY(-1px); } /* ── FOOTER ──────────────────────────────── */ footer { text-align: center; padding: 36px 24px; font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 11px; letter-spacing: 2px; color: var(--muted); border-top: 1px solid var(--rule); } /* ── ANIMATIONS ──────────────────────────── */ @keyframes fadeUp { from { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(16px); } to { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); } } .reveal { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(20px); transition: opacity 0.7s ease, transform 0.7s ease; } .reveal.visible { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); } /* ── RESPONSIVE ──────────────────────────── */ @media (max-width: 680px) { header, .section-wrap { padding-left: 24px; padding-right: 24px; } nav { padding: 0 24px; } .header-inner { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } .photo-block { grid-column: 1; margin-top: 36px; } .poems-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } } /* ── COLLAPSIBLE POEMS ───────────────────── */ .poem-card { cursor: default; } .poem-toggle { display: flex; align-items: baseline; gap: 16px; cursor: pointer; user-select: none; } .poem-toggle .poem-title { margin-bottom: 0; } .toggle-icon { font-family: 'Jost', sans-serif; font-size: 10px; letter-spacing: 2px; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--lavender-dk); opacity: 0.7; transition: opacity 0.2s; white-space: nowrap; margin-top: 6px; } .poem-toggle:hover .toggle-icon { opacity: 1; } .poem-body { overflow: hidden; max-height: 0; transition: max-height 0.5s ease, opacity 0.4s ease; opacity: 0; } .poem-body.open { max-height: 2000px; opacity: 1; }

Poet  ·  German-American Voice

Nanette Schieron

Nanette Schieron is a German-American poet whose work explores memory, loss, resilience, and the experience of building a life between countries. A former psychotherapist, she brings both lyrical precision and deep human attentiveness to themes of identity, nature, and the enduring power of the past.

Her poems have appeared in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily and Haikuniverse, and she has been one of several featured poets at the Duxbury Art Complex Museum in Duxbury, MA — writing and reading original poems before an audience for four consecutive years. Nanette has been a featured poet at the Rockland Memorial Library, Rockland, MA, in addition to reading her work at other local libraries.

She writes with a close attentiveness to the world — its textures, its losses, and its unexpected moments of grace.

  • Poems
  • Readings
  • Themes
  • Contact

Selected Work

The Lavender Dress

Read ↓

Memory  ·  Personal History

My hands shaking, I brushed my hair, shadowed my eyes then slipped on the lavender dress — purple poppies blooming on soft Indian cotton — a dress too delicate for heartbreak.
It was my thirtieth birthday. My gold wedding band flashing in late afternoon sun, I drove the wildflower-meadowed country road north — the one that once led me home to you.
Steeped in summersweet, the breeze carried the memory of a first kiss — how you asked, how I said yes, our shyness giving way to desire with the press of our lips.
Still in reverie, I entered the waiting room. You looked up — no happy birthday wish no comment on the dress I wore for you. Your face, a gray wall; those deep blue eyes like locked windows before a storm.
You left the hour early, taking all the air with you. I called you coward, gasped for breath between sobs — cheeks burning. The counselor, her eyes soft handed me a tissue box.
The lavender dress tied at my nape felt suddenly tight — poppies, damp and dark, holding wordless sorrow.
My tear-streaked face met the cool night air — Above me a violet-blue sky opened — I drew a long, slow breath, started the car — as the moon rose spilling its silver light on the road ahead.

A version of this poem was published in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily.

Invitation

Read ↓

Nature  ·  Ekphrastic

Walk with me this warm spring evening as twilight washes into dusk, shrouding the river's blue with a deep pewter hue, across the burgeoning grasses, a heron's raspy call.
Come — we'll wander the tidal flats, follow mysterious animal tracks as our footsteps sink into the spongy layers. We'll drink in the long breath of eons that mingles with our own.
The moon will rise later tonight, silver stars already sequin the water's darkening skin, catching in the crowns of wind-blown pines.
Above, the hunter with his bow, watches over us, as the modern world drifts by.

Abecedarian Haiku

Read ↓

Form  ·  Nature

Readings & Performances

  • Duxbury Art Complex Museum

    Duxbury, MA  ·  Ekphrastic poems written and performed before a live audience  ·  Four consecutive years

  • Local Library Reading Series

    Several readings at libraries throughout the region

Poetry Themes

Memory Loss Resilience Immigration & Identity The Natural World Marriage & Relationships Dreams Ekphrasis The German-American Experience

Get in Touch

Readings, collaborations,
and correspondence welcome.

For inquiries about readings, ekphrastic commissions, or to connect about the work, please reach out by email.

Write to Nanette

© 2025 Nanette Schieron  ·  All poems remain the property of the author

pointer-events: none; } .header-inner { max-width: 860px; margin: 0 auto; position: relative; display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr auto; column-gap: 56px; align-items: start; } .header-text { grid-column: 1; } .photo-block { grid-column: 2; grid-row: 1; align-self: center; opacity: 0; animation: fadeUp 0.9s 0.6s forwards; } .photo-placeholder { width: 190px; height: 230px; border-radius: 4px; background: linear-gradient(160deg, #ddd5eb, #c9b8d8); Poet  ·  German-American Voice

Nanette Schieron

Nanette Schieron is a German-American poet whose work explores memory, loss, resilience, and the experience of building a life between countries. A former psychotherapist, she brings both lyrical precision and deep human attentiveness to themes of identity, nature, and the enduring power of the past.

Her poems have appeared in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily and Haikuniverse, and she has been one of several featured poets at the Duxbury Art Complex Museum in Duxbury, MA — writing and reading original poems before an audience for four consecutive years. She has also read her work at libraries throughout the region.

She writes with a close attentiveness to the world — its textures, its losses, and its unexpected moments of grace.

  • Poems
  • Readings
  • Themes
  • Contact

Selected Work

The Lavender Dress

Read ↓

Memory  ·  Personal History

My hands shaking, I brushed my hair, shadowed my eyes then slipped on the lavender dress — purple poppies blooming on soft Indian cotton — a dress too delicate for heartbreak.
It was my thirtieth birthday. My gold wedding band flashing in late afternoon sun, I drove the wildflower-meadowed country road north — the one that once led me home to you.
Steeped in summersweet, the breeze carried the memory of a first kiss — how you asked, how I said yes, our shyness giving way to desire with the press of our lips.
Still in reverie, I entered the waiting room. You looked up — no happy birthday wish no comment on the dress I wore for you. Your face, a gray wall; those deep blue eyes like locked windows before a storm.
You left the hour early, taking all the air with you. I called you coward, gasped for breath between sobs — cheeks burning. The counselor, her eyes soft handed me a tissue box.
The lavender dress tied at my nape felt suddenly tight — poppies, damp and dark, holding wordless sorrow.
My tear-streaked face met the cool night air — Above me a violet-blue sky opened — I drew a long, slow breath, started the car — as the moon rose spilling its silver light on the road ahead.

N. Schieron

A version of this poem was published in Autumn Sky Poetry Daily.

Invitation

Read ↓

Nature  ·  Ekphrastic

Walk with me this warm spring evening as twilight washes into dusk, shrouding the river's blue with a deep pewter hue, across the burgeoning grasses, a heron's raspy call.
Come — we'll wander the tidal flats, follow mysterious animal tracks as our footsteps sinking into the spongy layers. We'll drink in the long breath of eons that mingles with our own.
The moon rises later tonight, silver stars already sequin the water's darkening skin, catching in the crowns of wind-blown pines.
Above, the hunter with his bow, watches over us, as the modern world drifts by.

N. Schieron  ·  9.21.25
